title: Flat Freckles on Your Cat’s Iris: Understanding Iris Melanosis
meta_desc: Learn about flat brown spots on your cat's eye. Discover why iris melanosis is usually low urgency but requires professional monitoring.
content_html:
What Are These Flat Freckles?

Flat, brown pigment spots on the colored part of a cat's eye are most commonly a condition known as iris melanosis. This occurs when pigment-producing cells called melanocytes multiply on the surface of the iris. These spots typically appear as smooth, flat patches that do not change the texture of the eye. While the freckles themselves are benign, they represent a localized increase in pigment that needs to be monitored by a professional.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for flat iris freckles is Low. This is not an immediate medical emergency, and your cat is likely not in any pain. However, because benign melanosis can occasionally transform into iris melanoma—a serious and potentially invasive form of cancer—it is important to schedule a routine, non-emergency checkup. A veterinarian will use specialized tools to ensure the spots are flat and not affecting the internal pressure of the eye.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking clear, high-resolution photos of your cat's eye is one of the most effective ways to manage this condition. Because changes in iris pigmentation happen very slowly over months or years, it is impossible to track growth by memory alone. A baseline photo allows you and your vet to compare the size, shape, and number of freckles over time. If a photo shows the spots are becoming raised, lumpy, or causing the pupil to become irregular, the urgency level increases significantly.

faqs.question: Will these spots affect my cat's vision?
faqs.answer: In the early stages of iris melanosis, vision is typically unaffected. If the condition progresses to melanoma or causes secondary issues like glaucoma, vision could be impacted.

faqs.question: Can iris freckles be cured or removed?
faqs.answer: There is no 'cure' for iris melanosis, and the spots generally do not go away. Treatment is usually unnecessary unless the spots turn into malignant melanoma.

faqs.question: When should I be concerned about the spots?
faqs.answer: You should seek veterinary advice if the spots look raised (3D), if the eye appears red or cloudy, or if your cat begins squinting and showing signs of discomfort.
